questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion (the Wiener process) $\{B(t)\}$, what is the value of $B(0)$?
questions.explanation: Standard Brownian motion starts at the origin by definition: $B(0) = 0$ with probability $1$. The process then accumulates random fluctuations as time advances, but its initial value is fixed at zero. The other options confuse the starting point with later behaviour. Pinning $B(0) = 0$ is the first defining axiom.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, $B(t)$ has which distribution?
questions.explanation: By definition $B(t) \sim N(0, t)$: mean zero and variance equal to the elapsed time $t$. The spread widens with time, since the standard deviation is $\sqrt{t}$. The exponential and uniform options do not match the Gaussian structure of the Wiener process. Mean zero with variance growing linearly in time is the signature of standard Brownian motion.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: What is $\text{Var}(B(4))$ for standard Brownian motion?
questions.explanation: Since $B(t) \sim N(0, t)$, the variance is $t$, so $\text{Var}(B(4)) = 4$. The value $2$ is the standard deviation $\sqrt{4}$, and $16$ wrongly squares $t$. Variance equals the time itself for standard Brownian motion.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, what is $E[B(t)]$ for any $t > 0$?
questions.explanation: The Wiener process has mean zero at every time: $E[B(t)] = 0$. The variance grows as $t$, but the mean stays put at the origin. The values $t$ and $\sqrt{t}$ describe the variance and standard deviation, not the mean. A driftless Brownian motion has no systematic tendency up or down.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, the increment $B(t) - B(s)$ with $t > s$ has which distribution?
questions.explanation: Increments of Brownian motion are normal with mean zero and variance equal to the length of the time interval: $B(t) - B(s) \sim N(0, t - s)$. The variance depends only on the gap $t - s$, not on where the interval sits. Adding the times or shifting the mean misstates this stationary-increment property.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: What is $\text{Var}(B(5) - B(2))$ for standard Brownian motion?
questions.explanation: The increment $B(5) - B(2) \sim N(0, 5 - 2)$, so its variance is $5 - 2 = 3$. The value $7$ adds the times, and $10$ multiplies them. Only the difference of the endpoints sets the variance of an increment.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Brownian motion has independent increments. What does this mean?
options: All increments have the same value
Increments over disjoint (non-overlapping) time intervals are independent
Increments over overlapping intervals are independent
questions.explanation: Independent increments means that for non-overlapping time intervals, the corresponding increments of the process are independent random variables. Overlapping intervals share part of the path and are generally dependent. The value $B(t)$ certainly depends on $t$, and increments are random, not constant. Independence applies precisely to disjoint intervals.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, what is the covariance $\text{Cov}(B(s), B(t))$ for $s \le t$?
questions.explanation: The covariance function of standard Brownian motion is $\text{Cov}(B(s), B(t)) = \min(s, t)$, which equals $s$ when $s \le t$. This follows because $B(t) = B(s) + (B(t) - B(s))$ and the increment is independent of $B(s)$, leaving $\text{Var}(B(s)) = s$. The product $st$ and the difference $t - s$ are the wrong combinations. The smaller of the two times governs the covariance.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: What is $\text{Cov}(B(2), B(5))$ for standard Brownian motion?
questions.explanation: Using $\text{Cov}(B(s), B(t)) = \min(s, t)$, we get $\text{Cov}(B(2), B(5)) = \min(2, 5) = 2$. The value $5$ takes the larger time, $10$ multiplies them, and $3$ is the increment variance $5 - 2$. The minimum of the two times, here $2$, is the covariance.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Standard Brownian motion can be obtained as a scaling limit of which discrete process?
options: A simple symmetric random walk, suitably rescaled in space and time
questions.explanation: Brownian motion arises as the limit of a simple symmetric random walk when steps shrink and are taken faster, with space scaled by the square root of the time scaling; this is the content of the functional central limit theorem (Donsker's theorem). A deterministic trend has no randomness, and the Poisson process is a jump counting process, not a continuous Gaussian limit. The random-walk limit explains why increments are Gaussian.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Geometric Brownian motion is commonly used to model which quantity?
options: Interest rates that can go negative
questions.explanation: Geometric Brownian motion $S(t) = S(0)\exp\left((\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2)t + \sigma B(t)\right)$ is the standard model for stock prices because it remains strictly positive and has lognormally distributed values. Ordinary Brownian motion can go negative, which is unsuitable for prices. Counts and coin flips are discrete, not continuous positive paths. Exponentiating Brownian motion keeps the modelled price above zero.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For geometric Brownian motion $S(t) = S(0)\exp\left((\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2)t + \sigma B(t)\right)$, the log-return $\ln\!\left(\tfrac{S(t)}{S(0)}\right)$ has which distribution?
options: Normal with mean $(\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2)t$ and variance $\sigma^2 t$
questions.explanation: Taking logs, $\ln(S(t)/S(0)) = (\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2)t + \sigma B(t)$, which is a linear function of the normal $B(t)$, hence normal with mean $(\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2)t$ and variance $\sigma^2 t$. The price $S(t)$ itself is lognormal, but its logarithm is normal. Confusing the price with its log is the common slip here.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, what is $P(B(1) > 0)$?
questions.explanation: Since $B(1) \sim N(0, 1)$ is symmetric about zero, $P(B(1) > 0) = \tfrac{1}{2}$. The mean is zero, so positive and negative values are equally likely. The values $0$ and $1$ would require the process to be deterministic. Symmetry of the centred normal gives a half.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: What is the standard deviation of $B(9)$ for standard Brownian motion?
questions.explanation: The variance is $\text{Var}(B(9)) = 9$, so the standard deviation is $\sqrt{9} = 3$. The value $9$ is the variance itself, and $81$ squares it. The standard deviation of $B(t)$ is $\sqrt{t}$, here $\sqrt{9} = 3$.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Why are the sample paths of Brownian motion continuous but nowhere differentiable?
options: They have no jumps, yet wiggle too violently at every scale to have a tangent
They are smooth curves with well-defined slopes
questions.explanation: Brownian paths are continuous (no jumps), but on every interval they oscillate so erratically that no derivative exists at any point; the increment over a small time $h$ scales like $\sqrt{h}$, so difference quotients blow up. They are neither smooth, nor made of jumps, nor piecewise linear. Continuity together with extreme local roughness is the defining paradox of Brownian paths.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, what is $\text{Var}(B(3) - B(1))$?
questions.explanation: The increment $B(3) - B(1) \sim N(0, 3 - 1)$, so its variance is $3 - 1 = 2$. The value $4$ adds the times and $3$ takes only the larger endpoint. The variance of an increment is the length of its interval.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Brownian motion with drift is defined as $X(t) = \mu t + \sigma B(t)$. What is $E[X(t)]$?
questions.explanation: Since $E[B(t)] = 0$, taking expectations gives $E[X(t)] = \mu t + \sigma \cdot 0 = \mu t$. The drift term $\mu t$ supplies the mean, while the random part contributes only to the variance $\sigma^2 t$. The value $0$ would hold only without drift, and $\sigma^2 t$ is the variance. Drift sets the mean of the process.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Brownian motion with drift $X(t) = \mu t + \sigma B(t)$ has which variance at time $t$?
questions.explanation: The drift $\mu t$ is deterministic and adds no variance, while $\sigma B(t)$ has variance $\sigma^2 \text{Var}(B(t)) = \sigma^2 t$. So $\text{Var}(X(t)) = \sigma^2 t$. The value $\sigma t$ forgets to square the volatility, and the drift does not enter the variance at all. Only the diffusion coefficient, squared, scales the variance.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For geometric Brownian motion with $S(0) = 100$, $\mu = 0.1$, and any volatility $\sigma$, the expected price is $E[S(t)] = S(0)e^{\mu t}$. What is $E[S(2)]$?
questions.explanation: The mean of geometric Brownian motion is $E[S(t)] = S(0)e^{\mu t}$, independent of $\sigma$. Here $E[S(2)] = 100 e^{0.1 \times 2} = 100 e^{0.2} \approx 122.14$. The value $120$ uses simple growth $100(1 + 0.2)$, and $100 e^{0.1}$ uses $t = 1$. Continuous compounding at the drift rate gives the exponential factor $e^{\mu t}$.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Brownian motion has stationary increments. Which statement captures this?
options: The process returns to zero at integer times
The variance of $B(t)$ does not change with $t$
The distribution of $B(t + h) - B(t)$ depends only on $h$, not on $t$
questions.explanation: Stationary increments means the law of an increment depends only on the elapsed time $h$: $B(t + h) - B(t) \sim N(0, h)$ regardless of the starting time $t$. The increments are random, not zero, and $\text{Var}(B(t)) = t$ does grow with $t$, so that option is false. Stationarity refers to increments over equal time spans sharing the same distribution.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, what is $P(B(4) < 0)$?
questions.explanation: Because $B(4) \sim N(0, 4)$ is symmetric about zero, the probability of a negative value is $\tfrac{1}{2}$. The variance scales the spread but not the symmetry, so half the distribution lies below zero. Values $0$ and $1$ would require determinism. Symmetry of the centred normal makes negative and positive equally likely.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: The correlation between $B(s)$ and $B(t)$ for $s \le t$ equals $\sqrt{s/t}$. What is the correlation between $B(1)$ and $B(4)$?
questions.explanation: Using $\text{Corr}(B(s), B(t)) = \sqrt{s/t}$, we get $\sqrt{1/4} = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$. This comes from $\text{Cov} = s = 1$ divided by $\sqrt{s}\sqrt{t} = \sqrt{1}\sqrt{4} = 2$. The value $0.25$ is $s/t$ without the square root, and a correlation cannot exceed $1$. The square root of the ratio of times gives $0.5$.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Which of the following is a defining property of the standard Wiener process?
questions.explanation: Continuity of sample paths is one of the defining properties of the Wiener process, alongside $B(0) = 0$, independent increments, and $B(t) - B(s) \sim N(0, t - s)$. Increments are Gaussian, not exponential, the path is not monotone (it fluctuates up and down), and it takes real, not integer, values. Almost-sure path continuity is part of the definition.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: If $B(t)$ is standard Brownian motion, what is the distribution of $B(2) + B(2)$, that is $2B(2)$?
questions.explanation: Scaling a normal by a constant scales its variance by the square: $\text{Var}(2B(2)) = 2^2 \text{Var}(B(2)) = 4 \times 2 = 8$, and the mean stays $0$, so $2B(2) \sim N(0, 8)$. Note this is not the same as $B(2) + B(4)$ for distinct increments; it is twice one value. The value $N(0, 4)$ forgets to use $\text{Var}(B(2)) = 2$, and $N(0, 2)$ ignores the factor $2$ entirely. Multiplying by $2$ quadruples the variance from $2$ to $8$.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: In the random-walk approximation to Brownian motion, a walk takes steps of size $\Delta x$ every $\Delta t$. To obtain a nondegenerate limit, how must $\Delta x$ scale with $\Delta t$?
questions.explanation: For the rescaled random walk to converge to Brownian motion, steps must scale as $\Delta x \propto \sqrt{\Delta t}$, so that the variance accumulated per unit time stays finite (variance per step $\propto \Delta x^2 \propto \Delta t$). If $\Delta x \propto \Delta t$ the fluctuations vanish, giving a deterministic limit, and a constant step diverges. The square-root scaling matches the $\sqrt{t}$ growth of Brownian standard deviation.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Let $\{B(t)\}$ be standard Brownian motion. Recall its mean and variance structure.
questions.solution: The starting value is $B(0) =$ {{1}}. The variance of $B(4)$ is {{2}}, so its standard deviation is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $B(0) = 0$ by definition. $\text{Var}(B(4)) = 4$ since the variance equals the time, and the standard deviation is $\sqrt{4} = 2$.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, consider the increment $B(5) - B(2)$.
questions.solution: The increment is normally distributed with mean {{1}} and variance {{2}}. The fact that this variance depends only on the gap $5 - 2$ reflects that Brownian motion has {{3}} increments.
questions.explanation: $B(5) - B(2) \sim N(0, 3)$, so mean $0$ and variance $5 - 2 = 3$. Dependence only on the interval length is the stationary-increments property.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: For standard Brownian motion, use the covariance function $\text{Cov}(B(s), B(t)) = \min(s, t)$.
questions.solution: The covariance $\text{Cov}(B(2), B(5))$ equals {{1}}. The correlation $\text{Corr}(B(1), B(4)) = \sqrt{1/4}$ equals {{2}}.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Brownian motion with drift is $X(t) = \mu t + \sigma B(t)$ with $\mu = 0.3$ and $\sigma = 2$.
questions.solution: The mean $E[X(t)] = \mu t$ at $t = 5$ equals {{1}}. The variance $\text{Var}(X(t)) = \sigma^2 t$ at $t = 5$ equals {{2}}. The deterministic part that sets the mean is called the {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $E[X(5)] = 0.3 \times 5 = 1.5$. $\text{Var}(X(5)) = 2^2 \times 5 = 20$. The term $\mu t$ is the drift.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Geometric Brownian motion models a stock with $S(0) = 100$, drift $\mu = 0.1$, and volatility $\sigma = 0.2$.
questions.solution: The expected price $E[S(t)] = S(0)e^{\mu t}$ at $t = 2$ equals $100 e^{0.2} \approx$ {{1}} (rounded to two places). The mean of the log-return over one year, $(\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2)\cdot 1$, equals {{2}}. The price stays positive because the model takes the {{3}} of a Brownian motion.
questions.explanation: $E[S(2)] = 100 e^{0.1 \times 2} = 100 e^{0.2} \approx 122.14$. Log-return mean over one year: $0.1 - \tfrac{1}{2}(0.04) = 0.1 - 0.02 = 0.08$. Exponentiating Brownian motion keeps the price positive.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models

questions.stem: Brownian motion is the scaling limit of a simple symmetric random walk.
questions.solution: For a random walk with step size $\Delta x$ taken every $\Delta t$, a nondegenerate Brownian limit needs $\Delta x$ proportional to {{1}}. The limiting process $B(t)$ has continuous sample paths but is nowhere {{2}}. By symmetry, $P(B(1) > 0)$ equals {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Steps scale as $\sqrt{\Delta t}$ to keep variance per unit time finite. The limit is continuous but nowhere differentiable. With $B(1) \sim N(0,1)$ symmetric, $P(B(1) > 0) = 0.5$.
questions.source: in the style of Ross, Introduction to Probability Models
